title: Gradual information diffusion across commonly owned firms
abstract: This paper studies how common institutional ownership (CIO) affects information diffusion in the stock market. My findings suggest that CIO can exacerbate the slow spread of information across firms. With over 50% of institutional investors holding concentrated stock portfolios, I infer a fundamental connection among firms with CIO. These firms exhibit cross-predictability in monthly stock returns, leading to a CIO-based peer momentum strategy that outperforms Ali and Hirshleifer's (2020) shared-analyst momentum strategy. This anomaly stems primarily from institutional investors with fewer stock holdings, who employ passive asset management characterized by lower portfolio turnover and more delegated investment.

author.Jie Ying*: Department of Economics and Finance, Southern Illinois University Edwardsville, United States|||Vaughan Institute, Tippie College of Business, University of Iowa, United States

article.1. Introduction: Researchers test the hypothesis of gradual information diffusion in the stock market by examining the stock returns of economically related firms. A positive serial cross-correlation of stock returns1 has been found among firms that are related by industry and product market segmentation (e.g., Hou, 2007; Cohen and Lou, 2012; Hoberg and Phillips, 2018; Eisdorfer et al., 2022), supplier-customer chain (e.g., Cohen and Frazzini, 2008; Menzly and Ozbas, 2010), production technology (e.g., Lee et al., 2019) and geographic proximity (e.g., Addoum et al., 2020; Parsons et al., 2020). Investor inattention or information processing constraint is often considered as the friction that slows down the information diffusion process. Previous studies show that institutional investor ownership mitigates the pricing friction and expedites information diffusion, particularly when institutional investors simultaneously hold stocks that are economically related. For example, Cohen and Frazzini (2008) and Menzly and Ozbas (2010) find that stock return cross-predictability fades as common institutional ownership (CIO) emerges.Does common institutional ownership always lead to faster dissemination of information in the stock market? To answer this question, this paper examines the stock return cross-predictability of firms that are commonly owned by institutional investors. Using the holdings data extracted from Security and Exchange Commission (SEC) 13(f) filings over the 1980-2017 period, I show that firms with CIO tend to share more similar fundamentals than those in the same industry. This is because over half of institutional investors tend to hold concentrated stock portfolios (i.e., less than 60 domestic common stocks), which is consistent with van Nieuwerburgh and Veldkamp, 2009, van Nieuwerburgh and Veldkamp, 2010, who argue that investors should focus on a subset of stocks in which they have a comparative advantage of information. These firms, despite being owned by the same group of institutional investors, show significant cross-predictability in monthly stock returns. My research finds that a long-short arbitrage portfolio based on the average returns of other commonly owned stocks, defined as CIO peer momentum, outperforms portfolios based on industry and shared-analyst peer momentum.The analysis of economic mechanisms demonstrates that the gradual dissemination of information among firms that share common ownership is largely due to passive portfolio management by institutional investors. The calculation of CIO peer momentum gives more weight to the returns of stocks held by institutional investors who hold fewer stocks. These investors tend to have lower portfolio turnover and delegate more assets to external asset managers like exchange-traded funds (ETFs). The abnormal returns from the cross-predictability decline when institutional investors with more concentrated portfolios are removed from the sample. My research suggests that institutional investors with more diversified portfolios expedite the information diffusion process. However, the anomaly persists as institutional investors tend to only pay attention to the peer momentum within their own portfolios. I also find that institutional investors increase (reduce) holdings of undervalued (overvalued) stocks while disposing the stocks that are no longer fundamentally related.Compared to industry, market sector, and other businesses, the economic link among commonly owned firms is much more inconspicuous. Conceptually, most if not all institutional investors are bound by benchmark policy to diversify their portfolios. Common ownership should indicate no economic link if institutional investors hold a large number of stocks to achieve diversification or execute an index investing strategy. Nonetheless, SEC 13(f) filings show that the number of stocks directly held by institutional investors is far below what a diversified portfolio requires. As illustrated in Fig. 1, about a quarter of institutional investors in the United States held less than 20 domestic common stocks on average during the 1980–2017 period, while only another quarter held more than 140 stocks.Download: Download high-res image (74KB)Download: Download full-size imageFig. 1. The institutional investors who hold at least two stocks are included in the sample. The x-axis shows the bins for the time series average number of common stocks in institutional portfolios. The y-axis shows the number of institutional investors. Distribution of number of institutional holdings from 1980 to 2017.The concentration of institutional portfolios can be explained by theory and recent developments in financial markets. Theoretically, as argued by van Nieuwerburgh and Veldkamp, 2009, van Nieuwerburgh and Veldkamp, 2010, rational investors should concentrate on a set of assets whose value can be better assessed with the information advantage they possess. Institutional investors would incur more costs to cover a large bundle of unrelated firms than focusing on a small set of related firms, because they may need to hire more security analysts and conduct more industry research to invest in more unrelated firms. Institutional investors using passive investment vehicles also favor concentrated portfolios. For instance, exchange-traded funds (ETFs) offer institutional investors an effective yet less costly option for diversification and benchmark tracking. Fig. 2 shows a decline in the number of stocks held by institutional investors against their growing population. The number of institutional investors grows more than 8 times from 1980 to 2017, while the median number of stocks they hold declines from 100 to 60. Meanwhile, the average percentage of ETF holdings in institutional portfolios plunged from 1997 to 2017.Download: Download high-res image (579KB)Download: Download full-size imageFig. 2. Time trend of institutional investments from 1980 to 2017.One important consequence of institutional investors concentrating on a small set of stocks is that economically related firms or firms that operate in similar sectors or industries are commonly owned by the same group of investors. For example, from 2007 to 2017, 76.3% of the institutional investors who invested in Spirit AeroSystems (one of Boeing's main suppliers) also held shares of Boeing, while only 39.1% held shares of Papa John's.2To empirically test the implication of CIO for firms' fundamental connection, I use a novel measure of common ownership as the weighting scheme to examine the correlation between the fundamentals of a firm and the weighted composite of other commonly owned firms. I estimate the common ownership of any two firms using the weighted count of institutional investors of both firms with the weight being inversely proportional to the number of stocks each institutional investor holds. The weighting scheme puts a higher weight on common ownership by institutional investors who concentrate on fewer stocks. Two stocks with common ownership by more diversified institutional investors are less likely to be related. The common ownership by indexers inevitably adds noise to the identification of economic links but does not bias the results substantially.3 With the weighting scheme, the common ownership measure between Spirit AeroSystems and Boeing (0.368) becomes four times higher than the common ownership between Spirit AeroSystem and Papa John's (0.070).I find a series of significant correlations and lead-lag relations between commonly owned firms in regard to annual growth in sales, gross profit, research and development (R&D) expense, and total assets. The positive correlations remain economically and statistically significant after controlling for the SIC- and 10-K text-based (Hoberg and Phillips, 2016) industry trends, and the peer trend based on shared analyst coverage (Ali and Hirshleifer, 2020). The lead-lag relations are more robust between the commonly owned firms than between firms and their industry and analyst-linked peers, which suggests that institutional investors concentrate on more closely related firms than industry classification and analyst coverage.With the peer firms inferred from CIO, I document a CIO momentum effect, in which stock returns are positively correlated with the average prior-month benchmark-adjusted returns4 of stocks they share CIO with. I find that both value-weighted and equal-weighted arbitrage portfolios that rely on the CIO momentum generate sizable alphas—over 12% per annum—under various asset pricing models. Their cumulative returns show no sign of subsequent reversals throughout the following 12 months, which confirms the presence of gradual information diffusion instead of market correction to prior excessive comovement induced by portfolio reallocation (e.g., Anton and Polk, 2014; Gao et al., 2017).Spanning tests demonstrate a much stronger return predictive power of the CIO momentum than the industry and Ali and Hirshleifer's (2020) shared-analyst momentums, the latter of which has been shown to absorb all other peer momentums. Even though the CIO momentum and shared-analyst momentum are highly correlated, the former is better at identifying the best-performing stocks than the latter. The long-short strategy that relies on the CIO momentum yields significant alpha within the top quintile of stocks sorted by the shared-analyst momentum, but not vice versa. Conditional on a series of stock characteristics, the alpha generated by the arbitrage portfolio based on the CIO momentum stays consistent, while the alpha from the shared-analyst momentum strategy significantly declines with firm size and age, and increases with volatility and various measures of information asymmetry.The superior cross-predictability of fundamentals and stock returns among commonly owned firms suggests several methodological advantages of using CIO for peer identification. First, CIO captures the multidimensional and time-varying nature of the connection of peer firms. Institutional investors manage their portfolios based on their up-to-date information, which potentially covers multiple themes of the connection between firms. Hence, compared to industry classification and other static peer identifications, CIO is more comprehensive and dynamic. I show that stocks held by institutional investors on average have worse information asymmetry than stocks covered by analysts, which suggests that ownership by institutional investors is more attracted by the profitability of mispriced stocks than analyst coverage. Retrospectively, institutional investors' indexing and diversifying appear to induce less noise for peer firm identification than analyst coverage biases.5 Lastly, institutional investors can exercise their voting rights to reinforce the connection among commonly owned firms.In this study, I investigate the economic mechanism behind the gradual information diffusion among commonly owned firms from three different angles. First, I examine whether all institutional investors contribute equally to the cross-predictability of stock returns; if not, I try to determine which institutional investors play a more essential role. Second, following Cohen and Frazzini (2008) and Menzly and Ozbas (2010), I look into changes in institutional ownership in response to the CIO momentum and test the hypothesis that institutional investors' hold-ups of trading delay the information diffusion process. Lastly, I inspect the direct impact of institutional investors by conditioning the arbitrage strategy on firms' percentage of institutional ownership.I find that the cross-predictability of commonly owned stocks is primarily driven by institutional investors who concentrate on fewer stocks, under 150 in particular. Those more focused institutional investors are typically smaller asset managers who delegate more investments to ETFs and closed-end funds. The number of directly held common stocks is negatively associated with institutional investors' active weight (Doshi et al., 2015), which measures the deviation of portfolio allocation from the market index, the industry Herfindahl-Hirschman Index (HHI), and is positively correlated with portfolio turnover, measured by the churn rate (Gaspar et al., 2005; Yan and Zhang, 2009). Furthermore, mutual fund managers are less focused than other institutional investors. Focused institutional investors are more likely to be dedicated investors, as defined by Bushee and Goodman (2007), than transient investors and quasi-indexers.Institutional investors as a whole increase (decrease) their ownership of a firm with the presence of a good (bad) peer momentum. The change in institutional ownership is particularly sensitive to the momentum of other stocks that institutional investors also own, which is consistent with the findings in Cohen and Frazzini (2008) and Menzly and Ozbas (2010). However, my regression analysis based on the three-dimensional data structure (investor–date–stock) shows that institutional investors only trade a stock based on the momentum of peer stocks they also hold in their own portfolio, neglecting the peer momentum based on the common ownership of the other institutional investors. The selective attention of institutional investors likely causes delays in the full diffusion of relevant information carried in the peer momentum.In contrast to more focused institutional investors, I show that those who hold more stocks (i.e., less focused) are shown to expedite the information diffusion process among peer firms by changing their ownership in response to the peer momentum. I find that the long-short strategy yields significantly lower alpha from stocks with a higher percentage of ownership by less focused institutional investors. This finding reconciles this paper to prior studies that document the mitigation effect of CIO to the gradual information diffusion.One limitation of using a quarter-end snapshot of institutional holdings to form the CIO momentum strategy for the next quarter is that the snapshot does not take into account the stocks that institutional investors have studied yet not purchased. This limitation causes the returns of baseline quintile portfolios to be left skewed because the snapshot of quarter-end holdings includes overpriced stocks that institutional investors plan on selling in the next quarter, but does not capture the underpriced stocks that institutional investors plan on buying. To confirm this hypothesis, I split the common ownership measure for a stock by whether institutional investors increase or reduce their holdings of the stock in the previous quarter. The returns of quintile portfolios based on buy-CIO (sell-CIO) momentum are right-skewed (left-skewed). The long-short strategy fails when the selloff-CIO momentum is used, which suggests that institutional investors remove stocks when they consider them to be no longer related to the remaining holdings.Lastly, for robustness, I compare the baseline CIO momentum with several alternatives that are built with different weighting schemes and measures of peer firms' leading returns. For instance, I compare the arbitrage portfolio performance using CIO momentums calculated with equal and dollar weighting and gross returns instead of benchmark-adjusted returns. A spanning test shows that the baseline CIO momentum retains robust predictive power for the subsequent stock returns after being orthogonalized to the alternatives, but most of the alternatives' predictive power diminishes after I control for the baseline CIO momentum.This paper makes two main contributions to the literature. First, this is the first study showing that common ownership by some institutional investors could delay information diffusion among peer firms. Previous studies typically consider institutional investors to be more attentive and better informed than retail investors, thus a higher percentage of institutional ownership is expected to mitigate the delays of pricing value-relevant information (e.g., Yan and Zhang, 2009; Baik et al., 2010; Bartram et al., 2015; Boone and White, 2015). Consistent with this argument, researchers find common ownership by institutional investors expedites the information diffusion between peer stocks. For example, Hou (2007), Menzly and Ozbas (2010), and Lee et al. (2019) find a negative relationship between the magnitude of cross-predictability and the percentage of institutional ownership. More specifically, Cohen and Frazzini (2008) and Hoberg and Phillips (2018) show that common ownership of economically related firms by mutual funds expedites the information diffusion process. My findings demonstrate that only the less focused institutional investors (i.e., the ones diversifying with a larger number of stocks) mitigate the gradual information diffusion issue. Meanwhile, common ownership by more focused institutional investors worsens gradual information diffusion since those investors delay their trading.The second contribution of this paper is to provide new empirical evidence showing that a large portion of institutional investors concentrate on fundamentally related firms. There is a growing body of literature that reveals investors' preference and pecuniary incentives for simultaneously holding stocks that are connected by, for instance, industry (e.g., Kacperczyk et al., 2005, Kacperczyk et al., 2007; Fedenia et al., 2013) and location (e.g., Coval and Moskowitz, 1999, Coval and Moskowitz, 2001; Ivković and Weisbenner, 2005; Seasholes and Zhu, 2010; Choi et al., 2017). van Nieuwerburgh and Veldkamp, 2009, van Nieuwerburgh and Veldkamp, 2010 propose the information-driven concentrated portfolio theory, which rationalizes institutional investors' concentrating behavior. Institutional investors' need for diversification is fulfilled by delegating passive investments to external managers such as ETFs, which is consistent with the finding in DeVault et al. (2021).The remainder of the paper is organized as follows. In Section 2, I describe the data and methodology. In Section 3, I present the main results regarding the firms' fundamental correlation and the stock return cross-predictability inferred from their CIO. In Section 4, I scrutinize the economic mechanism. In Section 5, I describe the robustness checks. Concluding remarks are in Section 6.
article.2. Data and methodology: In this section, I discuss the data source and describe how the sample of stocks and institutional investors are selected. Additionally, I introduce a new measure of common ownership, which will serve as a weighting scheme to calculate the CIO-linked peer trend and the CIO momentum.2.1. Data and sampleI merge historical stock data from the Center for Research in Security Prices (CRSP) with financial statement data from Compustat. The Form 13F institutional investors holdings data comes from Refinitiv/Thomson Reuters. I extract analysts' coverage and forecast information from the Institutional Brokers Estimate System (IBES) maintained by Refinitiv/Thomson Reuters. I download the asset-pricing factors and Fama-French industry definitions from Kenneth French's online data library.6 The text-based industry classification comes from the Hoberg and Phillips Data Library.7 I download the institutional investor classification from Brian Bushee's personal website.8 I identify the mutual fund managers using the s12type5 file from the mutual fund holdings data provided by Infinitiv/Thomson Reuters. I download the data of the Probability of Informed Trading (PIN) computed using the Venter and DeJong model from Stephen Brown's website.9I examine institutional holdings from March 1980 to December 2017. institutional investors with less than two valid common stock holdings are excluded from the sample. I sample firms monthly from April 1980 to March 2018 since I first estimate the return predictor at the end of April 1980 based on the previous quarter-end institutional holdings. The firm sample is restricted to the U.S.-based issuers of common stocks (with share codes 10 and 11) that are traded on the NYSE, NYSE MKT (formerly AMEX), and NASDAQ, and held by at least one institutional investor. Stocks are removed from the sample in the regression analyses if their previous month-end price is missing or less than $5 or their book value of equity is missing from Compustat. In all the portfolio tests, I further exclude the micro-cap and nano-cap stocks that are in the bottom (10%) of the NYSE breakpoints.Panel A in Table 1 shows that the stocks that are held by at least one institutional investor comprise about 95% of all the stocks traded in the three exchanges in terms of market capitalization. Most of the missing stocks are the small-caps traded in the NASDAQ, possibly due to the restriction of excessive risk-taking for some institutional investors. The results in Panel B suggest a wide variation of institutional ownership across the sample firms. The number of institutional shareholders of the sample firms ranges from 2,563 to 1, with 29 being the median. On average, institutional investors own about one-third of a firm. Panel C shows that the median number of common stocks institutional investors hold is 78 and the mean is 196, both of which are significantly less than the number of constituents in most stock market indexes. I show that different types of institutional investors hold different numbers of common stocks. Dedicated investors on average hold the fewest stocks compared to transient investors and quasi-indexers who hold the most. The median number of stocks held by mutual funds is 209, while the other non-mutual fund managers tend to hold much fewer, about only a third of mutual funds'. Panel D reports the statistics on institutional investors' portfolio allocation to ETFs and closed-end funds. On average, 6.41% of institutional investors' portfolios are delegated to ETFs. Some institutional investors have a majority of their investments delegated to ETFs and closed-end funds.Table 1. Measuring common ownershipIn this study, I use the common ownership of two firms by institutional investors to infer the fundamental correlation of these two firms. van Nieuwerburgh and Veldkamp, 2009, van Nieuwerburgh and Veldkamp, 2010 theorize that it is optimal for investors to hold a concentrated portfolio that consists of firms whose value-relevant information the investors have a comparative advantage over acquiring and processing. Due to the information constraints and learning costs, only firms with similar fundamentals are included in the concentrated portfolio. Depending on the specific information advantage, an investor's concentrated portfolios may include firms that are connected in various ways, such as industry membership, supplier-customer relation, geographic proximity, etc. Therefore, the common ownership aggregated across all of the concentrated portfolios should represent a relationship network of firms from the investors' perspectives.10 Further, the extent of similarity in fundamentals between two firms should increase with the number of their common owners. To mitigate the measurement noises from institutional investors' practice of indexing and diversification, which makes institutional investors simultaneously hold fundamentally unrelated firms, I assign more weight to the common ownership by institutional investors who focus on fewer common stocks. In Section 5, I inspect several alternative common ownership measures that assign weight to institutional investors based on the dollar value they hold, the under-/over-weighting relative to the market index portfolio, and the past trading direction.The baseline measure of common ownership between two firms is described as follows: At the end of quarter q, suppose there are Nq institutional investors holding Mq firms. The overall ownership distribution can be expressed as the matrix below,(1)Oq=[o1,1,q…o1,Mq,q⋮⋱⋮oNq,1,q…oNq,Mq,q], where on,m,q=1 if investor n holds any positive shares of firm m by the end of quarter q and on,m,q=0 otherwise. The extent of common ownership between firm i and firm j is measured by ci,j,q, where:(2)ci,j,q=∑n=1Nq(on,i,q×on,j,q∑m=1Mqon,m,q−1). The numerator inside the bracket is the common ownership indicator, which equals 1 only if investor n owns both firms. The denominator calculates the number of other firms investor n owns in addition to either firm i or j. Equation (2) essentially calculates a weighted sum of all common ownership indicators for firm i and firm j. The common ownership indicator is more likely to reflect the fundamental connection between i and j if the common owner holds a more concentrated portfolio (i.e., fewer stocks). In the extreme case where investor n owns only firm i and firm j, the value of the fraction inside the bracket equals 1.Fig. 3 provides an example of how the common ownership is calculated. Assume there are four firms, A, B, C, and D, and three investors, 1, 2, and 3. Investor 1 holds firm A, firm B, and firm C; investor 2 holds firm A, firm C, and firm D; investor 3 holds firm A and firm B. The common ownership measure is 1.5 (=0.5+1) between firm A and firm B, 1 (=0.5+0.5) between firm A and firm C, and 0.5 between firm A and firm D. Hence I infer that firm B is the most connected to firm A, followed by firm C, and then firm D. Note that the common ownership is not necessarily symmetric; for instance, I infer that firm A is the most connected to firm C, but not vice versa.Download: Download high-res image (491KB)Download: Download full-size imageFig. 3. This figure provides an example of how I calculate common ownership. I assume that there are four firms, A, B, C, and D, and three investors, 1, 2, and 3. Investor 1 holds firm A, firm B, and firm C; investor 2 holds firm A, firm C, and firm D; investor 3 holds firm A and firm B. The common ownership measure is 1.5 (=0.5 + 1) between firm A and B, 1 (=0.5 + 0.5) between firm A and C, and 0.5 between firm A and D. Hence I infer that firm B is the most connected to A, C is the next, and D is the least. Note that the common ownership is not necessarily symmetric, for instance, firm A is inferred as being the most connected to firm C, but not vice versa. Illustration of common ownership measurement.The example above can also be used to illustrate the differences between my common ownership measure and some others in the literature. Anton and Polk (2014, p. 6) consider two stocks having greater common ownership if the common investors own a higher percentage of the total market value of both stocks. Consider the case in which firm D has the largest market value and is held almost entirely by investor 2 while the other three firms have much smaller market value and are equally held by their shareholders. Firm A thus has the greatest common ownership with firm D. Bartram et al. (2015, Eqns. 10 and 11) measure the common ownership by calculating the scaled Manhattan distance between both firms' institutional investors and then weighting it by their relative institutional holdings in dollars. According to the authors, firm C would be most connected to firm A if investor 2 holds most of firm A and puts more portfolio weight on firm C than she does on firm D. Gao et al. (2017) simply counts the number of common institutional investors, which results in the same extent of common ownership of firm B and firm C with firm A.I argue that my common ownership measure is more appropriate for studying institutional investors' influence on information diffusion among connected firms for the following reasons. First, my measure mitigates the adverse effect of institutional investors' incentives for diversification. Second, the inferred connection does not depend on institutional portfolio weights and the market value of commonly owned shares, thus is less impacted by contagion-related factors, such as exogenous fund flow shocks and portfolio rebalancing. Lastly, the asymmetry of the inferred connections better resembles reality.2.3. Trend and momentumFor each focal firm, I group its CIO-linked peers and build a portfolio with the weight being their previous quarter-end CIO to the focal firm. More specifically, I define the clustered trend of firm i's CIO-linked peers in quarter q as:(3)XCIO,i,q=∑j≠ici,j,q−1Xj,q∑j≠ici,j,q−1,i,j∈(1,2,…,M), where XCIO,i,q is firm i's CIO-linked peer trend of fundamentals, with Xj,q being peer firm j's fundamental indicator. When analyzing firms' annual fundamentals and their CIO-linked trends, I modify the common ownership measure by combining institutional investors' year-round holdings. The ownership indicator in Equation (1) equals 1 as long as the firm is held by the institution at least once during the year.I estimate firm i's CIO momentum using the average industry-adjusted Daniel et al. (1997) (DGTW) return of its CIO-linked peer firms. Since the CIO momentum is estimated every month, its definition varies slightly from Equation (3):(4)RCIO,i,t=∑j≠ici,j,q−1RDGTW,j,t∑j≠ici,j,q−1,i,j∈(1,2,…,M), where RDGTW,j,t is firm j's DGTW return in month t of quarter q.Different from prior studies that use gross stock returns of peer firms to measure peer momentum, I postulate that the benchmark-adjusted returns are the better proxies for firm-specific news.11 Moreover, using the benchmark-adjusted returns further alleviates the concern of institutional investors holding unrelated index constituents because the index constituents tend to be large-cap firms whose realized returns more closely follow their value-weighted benchmark returns. The contribution of those index constituents to the estimation of the CIO momentum thus becomes more trivial. I show in the robustness test that the CIO momentum effect becomes weaker if using gross returns.
article.3. Main results: In this section, I present the main findings of the study. Firstly, I examine the fundamental connection between commonly owned firms using panel regressions with fixed effects. After that, I investigate the performance of a CIO-based peer momentum strategy to determine whether cross-predictability exists. Finally, I compare the predictive power of CIO momentum with other peer momentums, specifically Ali and Hirshleifer's (2020) shared-analyst momentum.3.1. Firms' fundamental connectionDespite being supported by the theoretical developments in van Nieuwerburgh and Veldkamp, 2009, van Nieuwerburgh and Veldkamp, 2010, it is not immediately clear how well the CIO resembles firms' fundamental connection. Neither can the literature conclude whether the documented portfolio concentration is driven by institutional investors' information advantage or some systematic behavioral bias. Korniotis and Kumar (2013) explore the same question with a sample of retail investors and find the existence of both driving forces.To empirically verify the fundamental connection among commonly owned firms, I run both a contemporaneous and a predictive regression of a series of firms' annual financial statement variables on their respective CIO-linked trends, the estimation procedure is described in Section 2.3. I focus on the growth of four key fundamental indicators: sales, gross profit, R&D expenses, and total assets. All of the regressions include the growth variables to avoid the persistence issue of the level variables.I also regress annual fundamental growth onto two industry trends and Ali and Hirshleifer's (2020) analyst-linked trend. The first industry trend is based on Fama-French 48 industry classification and the second one is based on Hoberg and Phillips's (2016) text-based industry classification. The Fama-French 48 industry trend is the equal-weighted average of the fundamental growth of firms in the same industries excluding the focal firm. The Hoberg-Phillips text-based industry trend uses the similarity scores between two firms as the weight of the connection. Following Ali and Hirshleifer (2020), the analyst-linked trend is the weighted average of peer firms' fundamental growth with the weight being the number of analysts who issue at least one FY1 or FY2 earnings forecast for both the focal firm and the peer firms in the same year. Due to the data availability, the text-based industry trend starts in 1989 and the analyst-linked trend starts in 1984.The results are reported in Table 2. I find that the CIO-linked trend significantly correlates with the focal firm for all four indicators of fundamental growth. For example, as shown in column (1) of Panel A, a 1% increase in the weighted average sales growth of the CIO-linked peers is associated with a 3.75% increase in the focal firm's sales growth. The positive association remains significant after I control for the industry trends and analyst-linked trend. Columns (3) and (4) of each panel report the results from the predictive regressions. I find that the CIO-linked trend is the only one that robustly predicts the future growth of the focal firm's sales, gross profit, R&D expenses, and total assets.Table 2. CIO momentum and return predictabilityPrior studies show that stock returns of the economically linked firms cross-predict each other as the market sluggishly integrates the peer momentum. In this subsection, I examine the return predictability using the CIO momentum to see whether it is related to the gradual information diffusion or some transient mispricing. I then compare CIO momentum to several industry momentums and the shared-analyst momentum. In all the portfolio tests, I exclude the firms whose previous month-end stock price is lower than or equal to $5 and whose market capitalization falls under the bottom (10%) of NYSE breakpoints, to ensure the results are not driven by these micro- and nano-cap stocks.3.2.1. Baseline portfolio testsI sort and group firms into quintiles at the beginning of each month based on their past CIO momentum estimated from the previous month. The top quintile group consists of the firms with the highest past CIO momentum and the bottom quintile group has the lowest past CIO momentum. I then construct either an equal-weighted or a value-weighted stock portfolio using the firms in each quintile. In addition, I form a self-financing arbitrage portfolio by long holding the top-quintile firms and short selling the bottom-quintile firms. All six portfolios are regrouped and rebalanced monthly. Each month, there are an average of 440 firms in each quintile portfolio over the formation period from April 1980 to March 2018.I evaluate portfolio performance using the portfolio's excess returns relative to the U.S. 3-month Treasury-bill rate, as well as the alphas from a batch of asset pricing factor models: CAPM, Fama and French's (1993) three-factor model, Carhart's (1997) four-factor model with and without short-term reversal factor, and Fama and French's (2015) five-factor model.The results in Table 3 show that both the excess return and alphas demonstrate that the firms with higher past CIO momentum significantly outperform the firms with lower past CIO momentum during the next month. The long-short value-weighted equity portfolio generates a monthly alpha of over 100 bps across all the asset pricing factor models. I find that the sizable risk-adjusted returns are not solely driven by the short-sale constraint as the long legs already generate monthly alphas that are over 0.5%. The negligible difference in returns between the value-weighted and the equal-weighted portfolios suggests that the portfolio profits I document are not biased by the inclusion of small-cap firms.Table 3. Comparison to shared-analyst coverageCIO is related to Ali and Hirshleifer's (2020) shared-analyst coverage. These authors consider that firms are fundamentally related if they are followed by the same group of sell-side analysts. Both Ali and Hirshleifer (2020) and this study implicitly identify the fundamentally related stocks through decisions made by investment professionals. There are two major differences in the motivation behind the decisions: (1) unlike institutional investors, analysts have little to no incentive to diversify the group of firms they cover; and (2) institutional investors have more straightforward pecuniary interests in the stocks they hold than analysts.I first examine the correlation between the peer momentums derived from CIO and shared-analyst coverage. Panel A of Table 6 illustrates the correlation between CIO momentum and shared-analyst momentum by showing the number of stocks in each of the 25 unconditionally double-sorted portfolios. If CIO momentum and shared-analyst momentum are perfectly correlated, all the stocks would be evenly sorted in the diagonal portfolios. There are noticeable discrepancies between the predictions of future stock performance based on non-extreme CIO momentum and shared-analyst momentum, despite both being largely similar in the top and the bottom quintiles. I show the accuracy of those predictions by estimating the 4-factor alpha of the 25 portfolios. In Panel B, the alphas suggest that CIO momentum and shared-analyst momentum have comparable predictive power for the worst-performing stocks; however, the CIO momentum is more accurate in predicting the best-performing stocks; the shared-analyst momentum fails to predict any best-performing stocks except for those the CIO momentum identifies as best performers. Panel C presents the results of conditional double sorting, in which sequential sorting is done within each initially sorted bin of stocks. article.4. Economic mechanism: In this section, I explore the reasons behind the delays in information diffusion among firms that are commonly owned. Firstly, I identify the group of institutional investors responsible for driving the CIO momentum effect and describe their characteristics. Secondly, I investigate how industry peer momentums, with and without CIO, affect institutional trading. Thirdly, I assess the impact of different institutional investors on the performance of the CIO momentum arbitrage portfolio. Lastly, I examine whether the CIO has a preference for undervalued stocks.4.1. Focused institutional investorsBy the construction of CIO momentum, institutional investors with fewer holdings are given more weight for the count of common ownership. Firms commonly owned by those investors are more likely to be fundamentally related, thus their stock returns contribute more to the CIO momentum. I identify which institutional investors essentially drive the CIO momentum effect and their motivation behind holding a concentrated portfolio.First, I employ a step-wise procedure to examine whether the number of holdings in institutional portfolios is relevant to the return prediction of the CIO momentum. I repeat the long-short arbitrage strategy described in Section 3.2.1 using stocks commonly owned by institutional investors who hold less than 20, 40, 60, ..., or 500 common stocks, respectively, and document the Carhart 4-factor (FF3M) alphas. As shown in Panel A of Fig. 7, the FF3M alpha peaks after I include the institutional investors who hold less than 150 common stocks. Common ownership by institutional investors who hold more than 150 stocks contributes little to the performance of the arbitrage portfolio. Excluding these more focused institutional investors gradually weakens the arbitrage performance. Panel B illustrates that the 4-factor alpha declines noticeably after excluding the common ownership of institutional investors who hold less than 150 stocks. These patterns reveal that the previously documented CIO momentum effect is primarily driven by the more focused institutional investors who invest in fewer stocks.Download: Download high-res image (487KB)Download: Download full-size imageFig. 7. Panel A: CIO Momentum by Including Less Focused Institutional Investors. Panel B: CIO Momentum by Excluding More Focused Institutional Investors. Number of institutional holdings and CIO momentum portfolio.To identify the focused institutional investors, I regress the log of the number of common stocks they hold onto their portfolio size, investment delegation, active asset allocation, industry concentration, portfolio turnover, and other identification categories. The results are in Table 7. After controlling for both institutional investor and quarter fixed effects, I find the number of common stocks held by institutional investors is positively associated with portfolio size, as measured by the market value, indicating that the more focused institutional investors are more likely to have smaller assets under management (AUM) and be more constrained by capital and other resources. Columns (2) and (3) show that the more focused institutional investors delegate more funds to external managers, such as ETFs and CEFs. This result is consistent with institutional investors' demand for diversification and benchmark tracking. Institutional investors can more closely follow fewer firms when their diversified portfolios are externally managed. The results in column (4)–(6) demonstrate that the more focused institutional investors are likely more strategic with their asset allocations, as measured by the deviation of their portfolio weighting from the market portfolio following Doshi et al.'s (2015) active weight, maintain higher industry concentration, as measured by the Herfindahl-Hirschman Index (HHI), and have lower portfolio turnover, as measured by the churn rate following Yan and Zhang (2009). In particular, the positive correlation between the number of institutional holdings and portfolio turnover suggests that the delays in information diffusion among stocks commonly owned by the more focused institutional investors may be due to the lack of active trading. Institutional trading and peer momentumBoth Cohen and Frazzini (2008) and Hoberg and Phillips (2018) argue that institutional investors expedite the information diffusion process by trading in the same direction as predicted by the peer momentum. To reconcile with these prior findings, I examine the trading pattern of institutional investors in response to the industry peer momentums with and without common ownership of peer stocks.For institutional investors to accelerate the information diffusion among peer firms, they need to evaluate the contemporaneous news of the peer firms, especially when they also hold stock in the peer firms. I test this hypothesis by regressing the split-adjusted quarterly change in percentage ownership by the institutional investors who hold the focal firm at the beginning of the quarter onto the cumulative momentums of the entire industry and the momentums of the industry peers that these investors simultaneously hold.To estimate the momentum of the commonly owned industry peers, I follow the same procedure as for the CIO momentum described in Subsection 2.3, except for using the gross returns of the firms in the same industry defined by the Fama-French 48 industry classification or the 3-digit SIC code. I include the lagged momentums in the regressions to control for institutional investors' momentum chasing behaviors. Since the institutional holdings are disclosed quarterly while the peer momentum effect is documented on a monthly basis, I estimate the lagged momentum by compounding the monthly momentum from the third month of the previous quarter to the second month of the current quarter. I also include the focal firm's quarterly compounded gross return and its lagged gross return to account for the ownership changes due to the focal firm's own news, as well as the firm and quarter fixed effects. I cluster the standard errors by both the firm and quarter.The results are in Panel A of Table 8. Consistent with the findings in Cohen and Frazzini (2008) and Hoberg and Phillips (2018), institutional investors, as a group, increase (decrease) their holdings of a firm when the firm's industry peers are under positive (negative) valuation shocks. The ownership changes respond to the momentum of industry peers that institutional investors commonly hold, instead of the entire industry, as the coefficients of the equal- or value-weighted industry momentum become insignificant once I include the commonly owned industry momentums in the regressions. These findings suggest that institutional investors trade on the news of the industry peer firms only if they hold them. The negative coefficient of the lagged Fama-French 48 industry momentum indicates that retail investors tend to follow the more coarsely defined industry momentum.Table 8. To further examine how each institutional investor responds to the news of commonly held industry peers, I run a three-dimensional panel regression at the investor-stock-quarter level. The dependent variable is the change in percentage ownership of stock i by institutional investor j over quarter q. I regress the individual ownership changes onto the momentum of stock i's industry peers held by institutional investor j (i.e., internal CO-industry momentum) and the momentum of stock i's industry peers that are commonly held by all other institutional investors (i.e., external CO-industry momentum). The separation of internal and external CO-industry momentums helps to identify what peer news elicits a response from institutional investors. In the three-dimensional panel regression, I control for investor-quarter fixed effects and stock-quarter effects, in which the former absorbs the effect from institutional investor-level changes, such as fund-flow induced rebalancing, and the latter absorbs the effect from stock-level changes, including stocks' returns and lagged returns. Standard errors are also clustered by investor-quarter and stock-quarter for robust t-statistics.Panel B of Table 8 presents the results from the three-dimensional panel regression. Individual institutional investors increase their holdings of a stock when the industry peers in their portfolio yield good returns, while reducing their holdings when the industry peers have bad news, which is consistent with the results at the stock level in Panel A. Nonetheless, the coefficient of the external CO-industry momentum suggests that institutional investors do not trade in the same direction as predicted by the industry peer momentum perceived by the other institutional investors. This finding suggests a selective attention bias of institutional investors. They only properly respond to the peer momentum based on their holdings, which slows down the diffusion of value-relevant information among the peer firms.4.3. Conditional influence of institutional ownershipIn Section 4.1, I show that the CIO momentum effect primarily exists among the stocks held by the more focused institutional investors who have lower portfolio turnover. While the results in Section 4.2 demonstrate that institutional investors overall trade in response to the industry peer momentum, it is not yet clear whether (1) institutional investors with different focusedness respond differently to the CIO momentum, (2) the influence of institutional ownership on the CIO momentum effect is conditional on the focusedness, and (3) the trading pattern and influence of institutional investors with different focusedness are consistent.To address the first question, I run a panel regression of the changes in the percentage of institutional ownership on the quarterly compounded CIO momentum. The control variables include the lagged CIO momentum, the focal stock's own quarterly gross return, and lagged return, as well as stock and quarter fixed effects. As shown in Panel A of Table 9, institutional investors change their holdings of stock in the same direction as predicted by the stock's CIO momentum. However, after differentiating institutional ownership by the extent of focus, I find that only the less focused institutional investors who hold the most tertile of common stocks quickly trade in response to the CIO momentum, while the other institutional investors are not significantly attentive towards the CIO momentum. This result is consistent with the positive correlation between the number of common stocks institutional investors hold and their portfolio turnover documented in Table 7 of Section 4.1.Table 9. I first sort sample stocks by their prior CIO momentum and percentage institutional ownership independently and group them into 25 portfolios. Among the five portfolios that are in the same institutional ownership quintile, I form an arbitrage portfolio by long holding the one with the highest prior CIO momentum and short selling the one with the lowest prior CIO momentum. I then estimate the 4-factor alpha of the five long-short portfolios and report the results in Panel B of Table 9. The alpha of the long-short portfolios declines with the percentage of total institutional ownership. However, the mitigating effect is primarily driven by the less focused institutional investors. Ownership by the more focused institutional investors has an insignificant influence on the portfolio alpha.The findings in Table 9 demonstrate that less focused institutional investors are more attentive and responsive towards the CIO momentum, thus their stock holdings expedite the information diffusion from the peer stocks. These more focused institutional investors delay trading the focal stocks despite the value signals from the peer stocks, which delays the peer information diffusion.4.4. Common ownership and stock undervaluationSection 3.2.4 shows that firms owned by institutional investors are surrounded by greater information asymmetry than firms followed by analysts. One possible reason why the CIO momentum portfolio outperforms the shared-analyst momentum portfolio is that institutional investors selectively hold stocks that are undervalued while analysts do not. I test this hypothesis in this section by examining the performance of the CIO momentum portfolio conditional on the prior change of ownership by institutional investors.The intuition is as follows: if an institutional investor adds a new position or increases its holding of a stock because it determines that the stock is undervalued, the peer momentum from this investor's portfolio is more likely to predict a positive future stock return. Meanwhile, if the institutional investor reduces its holding or entirely divests its position in a stock because the stock is overvalued, the peer momentum from its portfolio is more likely to predict a negative future return. Therefore, I create four subdivided CIO momentums based on the modified measures of common ownership, ci,j,q′, where:(6)ci,j,q′=∑n=1Nq(Δon,i,q−1×on,j,q∑m=1Mqon,m,q−1). The first subdivision of CIO momentum is when Δon,i,q−1 = 1 if institutional investor n increases its shares of stock i during quarter q−1, namely buy-CIO momentum. The second subdivision is when Δon,i,q−1 = 1 if institutional investor n adds a new position of stock i during quarter q−1, namely newbuy-CIO momentum. The third subdivision is when Δon,i,q−1 = 1 if institutional investor n reduces its shares of stock i during quarter q−1, namely sell-CIO momentum. The last subdivision is when Δon,i,q−1 = 1 if institutional investor n eliminates its holding of stock i during quarter q−1, namely selloff-CIO momentum.Table 10 reports the performance of the quintile portfolios sorted by these four subdivided CIO momentums. Compared to the baseline CIO momentum, the performances of the buy-CIO and newbuy-CIO momentum portfolios are much more right-skewed, which demonstrates that the stocks in which institutional investors increase their holdings tend to be more undervalued. By contrast, the sell-CIO momentum portfolios have more left-skewed performance, which indicates the overvaluation of stocks sold by institutional investors. None of the selloff-CIO momentum portfolios, however, yield any significant alpha. This result suggests that institutional investors eliminate their holding of a stock when the stock is no longer considered as peer to the other commonly owned stocks, hence the CIO momentum can no longer predict the future return of this stock.Table 10. article.5. Robustness checks: In this section, I compare the return predictability by the baseline CIO momentum with several alternatives of the estimated CIO momentum. I run a similar spanning test as in Section 3.2.3, which orthogonalizes the baseline CIO momentum and the alternative CIO momentums onto each other. I then compare the risk-adjusted returns of the long-short value-weighted portfolio formed with the orthogonal residuals.One major difference between the CIO momentum in this study and the other documented peer momentum, such as industry peer momentum (Hou, 2007) and shared-analyst momentum (Ali and Hirshleifer, 2020), is the measurement of peer returns. Most previous papers use the gross monthly returns while I use the benchmark-adjusted DGTW returns to alleviate the noises from institutional investors' index investing and the potential alpha attenuation problem with the gross return, which is pointed out by Burt and Hrdlicka (2021). Nevertheless, I create an alternative CIO momentum, ALT1, using the baseline weighting scheme and the gross returns.I estimate a second set of alternative CIO momentums, ALT2 and ALT3, by assigning equal weights to all the institutional investors when measuring the common ownership and using the benchmark-adjusted DGTW return and gross return, respectively. With equal weights, two firms are considered to share more common ownership when there are more institutional investors holding both firms. It is the same method used in Ali and Hirshleifer (2020) to count the number of shared analysts.For ALT4 and ALT5, I use the commonly held dollar value of two stocks as the common ownership measure, and use the peer stocks' DGTW return and gross return, respectively. My dollar value-based measure of common ownership is similar to Anton and Polk's (2014). If the CIO momentum effect is driven by the rebalancing-induced contagion, the return predictability should be more prominent with ALT4 and ALT5. At the end of quarter q, given Nq institutional investors and Mq firms, I define a new ownership matrix Dq,(7)Dq=[d1,1,q…d1,Mq,q⋮⋱⋮dNq,1,q…dNq,Mq,q], where dn,i,q equals the dollar value that institution n invested in stock i at the end of quarter q. I define a dollar value-based common ownership as ci,j,qd, and correspondingly, I calculate RALT4,i,t and RALT5,i,t for stock i during month t as:(8)ci,j,qd=∑n=1Nqdn,i,qdn,j,q(9)RALT4,i,t=∑j≠ici,j,q−1dRDGTW,j,t∑j≠ici,j,q−1d(10)RALT5,i,t=∑j≠ici,j,q−1dRj,t∑j≠ici,j,q−1d. The calculation of RALT4,i,t and RALT5,i,t assign heavier weights to the peer firms that are held more in dollar value by their common institutional shareholders, who also own more shares of the focal firm.Next, I evaluate the possibility that institutional investors' prior trades contain better information about firms' connections. ALT6 estimates the CIO momentum in which two firms share a higher degree of common ownership if they are both traded, regardless of whether they are bought or sold, by more institutional investors who make fewer trades in the last quarter. ALT7 is similar to ALT6 but considers the direction of trading—for each institution, the bought firms are considered to be inversely connected with the sold firms, thus the bad news of the sold firms serves as good news for the bought firms. The calculations of the common ownership and ALT6 and ALT7 are described below:(11)ci,j,qt1=∑n=1Nq(|tn,i,q×tn,j,q|∑m=1Mq|tn,m,q|−1)(12)ci,j,qt2=∑n=1Nq(tn,i,q×tn,j,q∑m=1Mq|tn,m,q|−1)(13)RALT6,i,t=∑j≠ici,j,q−1t1RDGTW,j,t∑j≠ici,j,q−1t1(14)RALT7,i,t=∑j≠ici,j,q−1t2RDGTW,j,t∑j≠ici,j,q−1t1, where tn,i,q=1 if institutional investor n increases its holding of stock i during quarter q, =−1 if decreasing the holding, and =0 if the holding does not change.The last set of alternative CIO momentums is related to the over- and under-weights of firms in the institutional investors' portfolios. Institutional investors may concentrate on the connected and noncompetitive firms by putting larger portfolio weight on them while giving lower weight to the rival firms, compared to the market index portfolio. I estimate ALT8 based on the common portfolio weight of two stocks relative to the market portfolio. Two firms are more likely to be positively connected if more institutional investors either overweight or underweight them both. Two firms are more inversely connected if institutional investors tend to overweight one and underweight the other. ALT9 ignores the direction of over- or under-weighting—two firms are considered to be more connected if more institutional investors weigh them more differently than the market index portfolio. article.6. Conclusion: Using SEC 13(f) filings between 1980 and 2017, I find that firms with CIO share similar fundamentals, such as annual growth in sales, profits, R&D expenses, and total assets. Institutional investors tend to concentrate their portfolios, which results in this similarity. It is more cost-effective for them to delegate the diversified portfolio to external asset managers, rather than cover more market sectors themselves. During the sample period, a quarter of institutional investors held under 20 domestic common stocks, while another quarter held more than 140.Previous studies suggested that CIO expedites the dissemination of information across fundamentally related firms, but my research indicates that the gradual information diffusion issue persists among firms that are commonly owned by institutional investors. I show that the stock return cross-predictability is more prevalent among commonly owned firms than firms in the same industry and firms covered by the same sell-side analysts. The CIO momentum strategy outperforms the industry momentum strategy and Ali and Hirshleifer's (2020) shared-analyst momentum strategy.The gradual information diffusion among commonly owned firms is primarily driven by more focused institutional investors who concentrate on fewer firms and delegate more funds to external managers. Common ownership by more focused institutional investors delays the information diffusion among peer firms by delaying trading in response to the peer momentum. Less focused institutional investors actively trade on the CIO momentum; however, their selective attention toward the fundamentally related firms within their portfolio fails to eliminate the CIO momentum effect.My results have the following implications for investors and policymakers: (1) investors could miss investment opportunities if they neglect the fundamental similarity of the commonly owned firms; (2) the passive investment strategy employed by some institutional investors who concentrate on fewer firms may impact market efficiency. For future studies, the similarity network of firms inferred from the CIO may be used to research the peer effect in corporate managerial decisions.
